title: DecodeEvent::Final variant is declared but never emitted by decode_progressive
description: The public enum `DecodeEvent<S>` has two variants: `Progress` and `Final`. The `decode_progressive` method and its internal driver `run_progressive_into` only ever call `on_event` with `DecodeEvent::Progress`; `DecodeEvent::Final` is never fired. The final decoded image is returned as `Ok(Image)` from `decode_progressive`, not via the callback. Any consumer that pattern-matches `DecodeEvent::Final` to receive the finished frame will never receive it, creating a silent gap between the documented shape of the enum and the actual runtime contract. The legacy borrowed API (`decode_progressive_frames_borrowed`) does correctly fire a `Final` event via its `DecodeProgressiveEvent::Final` variant.

title: bench_tone_stage_3way uses pre_r LUT for all three channels, producing wrong Stage-1 benchmark
description: In `bench_tone_stage_3way`, the Stage 1 pre-LUT gather loop reads all three channels (R, G, B) from `pre_r` instead of `pre_r`, `pre_g`, `pre_b` respectively. The function only builds `pre_r` (a single-channel LUT keyed on the R white-balance/gain). The G and B channels thus get wrong pre-LUT values since they have different WB gains. This makes the Stage 1 timing misleading (wrong access pattern vs production), and the function's documented contract — 'return (pre_lut_ms, tone_math_ms, post_lut_ms)' timing stages independently — is violated for G and B channels.

title: decode_jxl_rgba16 returns native-endian bytes but the doc says 'native-endian u16 packed as bytes' — no explicit endianness annotation for cross-platform consumers
description: The public function `decode_jxl_rgba16` uses `u16::to_ne_bytes()` (native-endian) and documents this as 'native-endian `u16` packed as bytes'. Any caller that deserializes the returned bytes on a different endianness platform (e.g. a big-endian decoder or a consumer that assumes little-endian) will misinterpret the pixel data. While the current codebase is x86-only (little-endian), the contract is ambiguous: the type signature `Vec<u8>` carries no endianness annotation, making this a footgun for future ports or heterogeneous environments. The sibling `decode_jxl_rgba8` has no such issue since u8 is endianness-agnostic.

title: process() and process_into() accept rgb16 of length not divisible by 3 in release builds
description: Both `process()` and `process_into()` assert `rgb16.len() % 3 == 0` via `debug_assert_eq!`, which is elided in release builds. An odd-length input will not panic but will silently produce a truncated output (the loop processes `len/3` pixels, discarding the trailing 0-2 samples) and — in the unsafe pointer-advance path — could be walked past the allocation boundary if `out.len() == rgb16.len()` but `rgb16.len()` is odd. The assertion should be a hard `assert!` (or `validate_pixel_buffer_u16`) on the public boundary.

title: FrameStats::luma_variance() divides by 65536 but luma_sum/luma_sq accumulate values scaled by (54+183+18)=255 per pixel, not 256
description: The per-pixel luma is computed as `l = 54*r + 183*g + 18*b` with r/g/b in [0,255]. The maximum value is 255*(54+183+18) = 255*255 = 65025, not 65536. The variance is then divided by 65536 to normalize to [0,1], but this divisor does not match the actual scale of the accumulated squared-sum — a correctly saturated white frame yields ~65025^2 / 65536 not 1.0. This makes `luma_variance()` return slightly incorrect values compared to what the doc comment implies (a [0,1] normalized variance). Callers comparing native values to WASM-side values (which may use a different scale) will see systematic drift.

title: perceptual_apply_bulk (c-perceptual) emits timing to stderr unconditionally on every call
description: The public function `perceptual_apply_bulk` records wall time and calls `eprintln!("perceptual_bulk_ms: {:.3}", elapsed_ms)` on every invocation, even in production release builds. This is a contract violation: a library function must not produce unconditional side-effects on the process's stderr; it is also a performance regression in tight loops (the Instant + format + write cost dominates for small n). The comment says 'hook for benchmarking' but there is no feature flag or cfg guard. Any caller expecting clean output (e.g. a server that parses stderr) will be corrupted.

title: decode_region silently drops extra channels from the result Image
description: The `decode_region` method decodes the full image (which does populate `extra` channels when `decode()` is called), then constructs a cropped `Image` with `extra: Vec::new()`. The public return type is `Image<S>` which includes an `extra` field, but for region decodes it is always empty even when the source stream contains extra channels. Callers that request a region decode and then inspect `img.extra` (e.g. to read a depth channel) will silently receive an empty slice rather than a slice of cropped extra-channel data. The docstring does not mention this limitation.
code_excerpt:
Ok(Image {
    width: w,
    height: h,
    channels: ch.count(),
    data,
    extra: Vec::new(),
    meta: full.meta,
})

category: enum handled by some consumers not others
title: DNG Cfa enum has no Rgb or Gray fallback arm — unsupported CFA patterns bail instead of degrading gracefully
description: The `decode_bytes` function matches the 4 known Bayer CFA patterns (RGGB, GBRG, GRBG, BGGR) and bails on all others. The downstream `demosaic` module exposes `demosaic_rggb` (the only bilinear/MHC implementation). If a DNG uses a CFA variant not in the list (e.g. X-Trans or a custom pattern), the error message is a raw byte-array debug print (`bail!("unsupported CFA pattern: {p:?}")`) with no information about which camera produced it. This is a usability/contract issue: the public `Cfa` enum documents four variants, but a fifth 'unknown' case is possible from the file format and the error surface is unhelpful to callers trying to decide whether to fall back.

title: ExifData.quality is Option<u8> but OrfInfo.quality is Option<u16> — truncation on cast
description: The `ExifData` struct declares `quality: Option<u8>` while the source `OrfInfo` struct stores `quality: Option<u16>`. The conversion in `from_orf_info` performs `info.quality.map(|q| q as u8)`, a silent truncating cast. Olympus quality tag values include standard JPEG quality levels (0–5) so values always fit in u8, but the contract mismatch means: (a) a future camera that uses larger quality values will silently truncate, and (b) the types themselves contradict each other, making it impossible for a reader to understand why u8 was chosen without inspecting both structs.

title: No round-trip contract test between decode_into and decode for extra-channel images
description: The `decode_into` public API explicitly documents that it does NOT read back planar extra channels ('planar extra channels are not read back — use decode for those'). There is no test that verifies the two functions are consistent for the same multi-channel input: a caller who switches from `decode` to `decode_into` for performance and relies on the extra channels will silently receive an empty `extra` field without any error. Adding a test that calls both on the same JXL and asserts `decode_into` returns `meta.num_extra_channels > 0` but empty extra data would document the intentional behaviour and catch any future regression.

title: JXTC container format has no version migration path beyond JXTC_VERSION=1 rejection
description: The JXTC header parser hard-rejects any container whose version field is not exactly `JXTC_VERSION (1)`. There is no mechanism for forward compatibility (unknown-but-future-minor versions) or graceful degradation. If a future writer bumps the version field to support new features, all existing readers will silently return `None` with no diagnostic. The tile-index schema, flag bits, and header layout are all unversioned within v1. This makes it impossible to extend the container without breaking all existing decoders.
